2008 American film directed by Frank Miller

***The Spirit*** is a 2008 American [neo-noir](/wiki/Neo-noir "Neo-noir") [superhero film](/wiki/Superhero_film "Superhero film") written and directed by [Frank Miller](/wiki/Frank_Miller "Frank Miller") and starring [Gabriel Macht](/wiki/Gabriel_Macht "Gabriel Macht"), [Eva Mendes](/wiki/Eva_Mendes "Eva Mendes"), [Sarah Paulson](/wiki/Sarah_Paulson "Sarah Paulson"), [Dan Lauria](/wiki/Dan_Lauria "Dan Lauria"), [Paz Vega](/wiki/Paz_Vega "Paz Vega"), [Scarlett Johansson](/wiki/Scarlett_Johansson "Scarlett Johansson"), and [Samuel L. Jackson](/wiki/Samuel_L._Jackson "Samuel L. Jackson"). Based on the [newspaper](/wiki/Newspaper "Newspaper") [comic strip](/wiki/Comic_strip "Comic strip") and [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics") character *[The Spirit](/wiki/Spirit_(comics_character) "Spirit (comics character)")*, by [Will Eisner](/wiki/Will_Eisner "Will Eisner"), and produced by [OddLot](/wiki/OddLot_Entertainment "OddLot Entertainment") and [Lionsgate Films](/wiki/Lionsgate_Films "Lionsgate Films"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) it tells the story of a ghost-like superhero who defends Central City from the Octopus, who competes with the superhero's childhood friend Sand Saref for the Blood of Heracles in order to become immortal.

The film was released theatrically in the United States on December 25, 2008, to negative reviews and was a [commercial failure](/wiki/Box-office_bomb "Box-office bomb"). It was later released on [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") on April 14, 2009.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Spirit_(2008_film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

Denny Colt, also known as, "The Spirit," learns about a major case from Detective Sussman involving his nemesis, "[The Octopus](/wiki/Octopus_(comics) "Octopus (comics)")". The Spirit dons his costume and travels across rooftops, saving a woman before connecting with Officer Liebowitz. At the swampland, [femme fatale](/wiki/Femme_fatale "Femme fatale"), Sand Saref, rises from the water to shoot Sussman. The Spirit and Liebowitz find the wounded Sussman. Sand and her husband, Mahmoud, had earlier fled with chests they recovered from the water. While being shot at, Sand escaped, leaving one chest behind which was retrieved by Octopus. The Octopus beheads Liebowitz, and his cloned henchmen attack the Spirit. His accomplice, [Silken Floss](/wiki/Silken_Floss "Silken Floss"), flees with the chest as the two arch-nemeses fight.

The next morning, the Spirit is awakened by his lover, [Dr. Ellen Dolan](/wiki/Dr._Ellen_Dolan "Dr. Ellen Dolan"), daughter of Commissioner Eustace Dolan. He is undeterred by his gunshot wounds, which have fully healed. He notices a gold locket in Sussman's hand, which had been torn from Sand's neck. The locket contains pictures of a much-younger Colt and Sand, and had been his gift to her. Sand had become disenchanted with the city's corruption, following the death of her father, a police officer, and left for fifteen years. In a secret lair, the Octopus and Silken Floss discover their chest contains the [Golden Fleece](/wiki/Golden_Fleece "Golden Fleece"), not the Blood of Heracles, as expected. Sand and Mahmoud visit an underworld figure who sold them the location of the treasure, and it is implied he gave the location to the Octopus.

Commissioner Dolan calls The Spirit away to a case and relates Sand's history as one of the world's great jewel thieves. While arresting her, he reveals he knows she is looking for the Golden Fleece, and she shoves him through a window, which he survives. The Spirit receives a tip on the location of the Octopus's lair, but is captured while investigating. The Octopus reveals that his and Floss's experimentation led to the creation of an immortality serum. The Octopus first tested it on Colt's dead body. Colt was revived and earned the ire of [Death](/wiki/Personifications_of_death "Personifications of death") for escaping her clutches. Eventually, the Octopus injected himself with the serum, but he needs the blood of the demigod [Heracles](/wiki/Hercules "Hercules") to perfect the formula. The Spirit escapes by seducing assassin Plaster of Paris, who as a parting gift turns on The Octopus. When the Spirit mentions Sand's name, she stabs him out of spite.

After recovering, the Spirit stumbles to the city docks and collapses into the water where Lorelei, the Angel of Death, confronts him. He initially submits, but changes his mind after remembering the women he has known. As he swims to the surface, she vows to have him. At the projects, Sand, Floss, and their henchmen meet to exchange the Blood of Heracles for the Golden Fleece. Sand attempts to convince Floss to leave the Octopus before he kills her. Floss gains the upper hand and the Octopus asks Floss for the vase of blood. As the Spirit suddenly materializes, Floss drives off, unable to take a side.

The Octopus shoots a series of progressively larger guns at the Spirit, apparently killing him, but Dolan's [SWAT](/wiki/SWAT "SWAT") team storms the area and opens fire. The Octopus is maimed. As he desperately tries to drink the Blood of Heracles, Sand shoots the vase. The Spirit rises, shown to be wearing a bullet-proof vest and blows up the Octopus with a grenade while Sand uses the Golden Fleece to protect them from the explosion.

The Spirit gives Sand her locket back. They kiss as Ellen looks on, feeling betrayed. The old flames bid each other goodbye and the Spirit convinces Dolan to release Sand in gratitude for helping to save the world. Nearby, Floss discovers one of the Octopus's severed fingers crawling towards her. She picks it up and departs with two of the clones. Meanwhile, the Spirit and Ellen make amends and embrace.

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Spirit_(2008_film)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast")]

* [Gabriel Macht](/wiki/Gabriel_Macht "Gabriel Macht") as [Denny Colt / The Spirit](/wiki/Spirit_(comics_character) "Spirit (comics character)"): An ambitious and formerly eager young cop killed on the job who under mysterious circumstances is reborn as a masked crimefighter with an eye for the ladies.[[5]](#cite_note-love-5) Determined to still keep his beloved city safe, he works with Central City's police force from the shadows.
  + [Johnny Simmons](/wiki/Johnny_Simmons "Johnny Simmons") as Young Denny Colt[[5]](#cite_note-love-5)
* [Samuel L. Jackson](/wiki/Samuel_L._Jackson "Samuel L. Jackson") as [The Octopus](/wiki/Octopus_(comics) "Octopus (comics)"): A former coroner turned psychotic supervillain who plans to bring all of Central City to its knees and will kill without discretion anyone unlucky enough to stand in his way.
* [Scarlett Johansson](/wiki/Scarlett_Johansson "Scarlett Johansson") as Silken Floss: A [femme fatale](/wiki/Femme_fatale "Femme fatale") secretary and perversely innocent accomplice to the Octopus, only slightly saner than he is.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* [Eva Mendes](/wiki/Eva_Mendes "Eva Mendes") as Sand Saref:[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Spirit's childhood sweetheart who perennially seduces and marries wealthy men, has them killed, and uses their money to fund criminal exploits in a constant pursuit of a life of the highest luxury and influence over the criminal underworld. She is also a tragic [anti-heroine](/wiki/Anti-heroine "Anti-heroine") with her policeman father accidentally murdered causing her to have a hatred of police and Central City and break up with aspiring cop Denny Colt. Her portrayal in the film combines her backstory from the original comics with characteristics of another prominent femme fatale in the series, P'Gell.
  + [Seychelle Gabriel](/wiki/Seychelle_Gabriel "Seychelle Gabriel") as Young Sand Saref
* [Sarah Paulson](/wiki/Sarah_Paulson "Sarah Paulson") as [Dr. Ellen Dolan](/wiki/Ellen_Dolan_(character) "Ellen Dolan (character)"): The police commissioner's daughter and a top surgeon who considers it her duty as the Spirit's current flame to keep him healthy and alive (much to her father's chagrin).[[5]](#cite_note-love-5)
* [Dan Lauria](/wiki/Dan_Lauria "Dan Lauria") as Commissioner Eustace Dolan: The hard-boiled and commanding police commissioner of Central City and the Spirit's father figure.[[5]](#cite_note-love-5)
* [Stana Katic](/wiki/Stana_Katic "Stana Katic") as Morgenstern: A spunky rookie officer and skilled sharpshooter who idolizes the Spirit and claims that Sand Saref suffers from [Electra complex](/wiki/Electra_complex "Electra complex").[[5]](#cite_note-love-5)
* [Louis Lombardi](/wiki/Louis_Lombardi "Louis Lombardi") as Phobos, Logos, Pathos, Ethos, Bulbos, Huevos and Rancheros, Mangos, Adios and Amigos, etc.: The Octopus's thuggish and moronic, yet highly resilient cloned henchmen.[[5]](#cite_note-love-5)
* [Jaime King](/wiki/Jaime_King "Jaime King") as [Lorelei Rox](/wiki/Lorelei_Rox "Lorelei Rox"): A phantasmic siren and the Angel of Death waiting to take the Spirit, who must continually force himself to resist her.[[8]](#cite_note-squad-8)
* [Paz Vega](/wiki/Paz_Vega "Paz Vega") as Plaster of Paris: A sexy French belly dancer and assassin in the employ of the Octopus, she wields tri-pronged throwing knives and a sword.[[8]](#cite_note-squad-8)
* [Richard Portnow](/wiki/Richard_Portnow "Richard Portnow") as Donenfeld: An underworld figure and fence of ancient artifacts that sold the map to the Blood of Heracles to Sand.
* Arthur the Cat as Himself: A cat that is the Spirit's animal companion.

[Frank Miller](/wiki/Frank_Miller "Frank Miller") and [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics") president [Paul Levitz](/wiki/Paul_Levitz "Paul Levitz") also have cameo roles in the film where Miller portrays Officer Liebowitz while Levitz portrays Onlooker #3.[[9]](#cite_note-fji-9)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Spirit_(2008_film)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Spirit_(2008_film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

In the 1970s, director [William Friedkin](/wiki/William_Friedkin "William Friedkin") obtained the film rights to *[The Spirit](/wiki/Spirit_(comics_character) "Spirit (comics character)")* and contacted [Will Eisner](/wiki/Will_Eisner "Will Eisner") to write a script for him. Eisner declined but recommended [Harlan Ellison](/wiki/Harlan_Ellison "Harlan Ellison"), who wrote a two-hour [live-action](/wiki/Live-action "Live-action") script for the filmmaker. Friedkin and Ellison afterward had an unrelated argument, and the project was abandoned.[[10]](#cite_note-10) During the 1980s, [Brad Bird](/wiki/Brad_Bird "Brad Bird"), [Jerry Rees](/wiki/Jerry_Rees "Jerry Rees"), and producer [Gary Kurtz](/wiki/Gary_Kurtz "Gary Kurtz") attempted to get an animated adaptation off the ground, though studio executives praised the screenplay, they thought the film would be unmarketable, and this version was scrapped.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In the early 1990s, producer [Michael Uslan](/wiki/Michael_Uslan "Michael Uslan") and executive producers [Benjamin Melniker](/wiki/Benjamin_Melniker "Benjamin Melniker") and Steven Maier subsequently obtained the rights for a live-action [film adaptation](/wiki/Film_adaptation "Film adaptation"). The producer promised Eisner that he would not permit anyone who "didn't get it" to work on the project. Two ideas pitched to Uslan were to put the Spirit in a costume and to have the Spirit be a resurrected dead man who possessed supernatural powers.[[13]](#cite_note-xtra-13) Screenwriter John Turman, a comic book fan, expressed interest in writing the script.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In July 2004, financier [OddLot Entertainment](/wiki/OddLot_Entertainment "OddLot Entertainment") acquired the rights to the film. OddLot's producers Gigi Pritzker and Deborah Del Prete began a collaboration with Uslan, Melniker, and Maier working at Batfilm Productions, to adapt the story. Eisner, who was protective of the rights to his creations, said that he believed in the producers to faithfully adapt *The Spirit*.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In April 2005, comic-book writer [Jeph Loeb](/wiki/Jeph_Loeb "Jeph Loeb") was hired to adapt *The Spirit* for the big screen,[[16]](#cite_note-16) but the writer eventually left the project. Later in April, Uslan approached [Frank Miller](/wiki/Frank_Miller "Frank Miller") at Will Eisner's memorial service in New York City several weeks after Miller's *[Sin City](/wiki/Sin_City_(film) "Sin City (film)")* was released in theaters, interested in initiating the adaptation technique with Miller's film for *The Spirit*.[[17]](#cite_note-panel-17) Miller had initially hesitated, doubting his skill in adapting *The Spirit*, but ultimately embraced his first solo project as writer-director.[[13]](#cite_note-xtra-13) Miller described his decision-making:

> The only thought in my mind was, "It's too big—I can't possibly do it." And I refused. And about three minutes later as I was at the doorway, I turned around and said, "Nobody else can touch this," and I agreed to the job on the spot.[[18]](#cite_note-filmjournal1-18)

In July 2006, the film [trade press](/wiki/Trade_journal "Trade journal") reported Miller would write and direct the film adaptation for *The Spirit*; Miller and the producers publicly announced this at the 2006 [Comic-Con International](/wiki/Comic-Con_International "Comic-Con International") in [San Diego, California](/wiki/San_Diego,_California "San Diego, California").[[19]](#cite_note-odd_job-19) Miller said that he was putting together a [film treatment](/wiki/Film_treatment "Film treatment") that included large parts of *The Spirit* strip panels. As Miller described the project, "I intend to be extremely faithful to the heart and soul of the material, but it won't be nostalgic. It will be much scarier than people expect".[[19]](#cite_note-odd_job-19) Miller filmed *The Spirit* using the same digital background technology that was used for *Sin City* and *[300](/wiki/300_(film) "300 (film)")*.[[20]](#cite_note-exclusive-20) The film would also copy specific shots from the comic, similar to *Sin City*.[[21]](#cite_note-nycc-21)

In February 2007, Miller completed the first draft of the screenplay and began work on a second draft.[[22]](#cite_note-popwatch-22) Principal photography was initially slated to begin in late spring 2007.[[19]](#cite_note-odd_job-19) Miller also planned to begin filming *[Sin City 2](/wiki/Sin_City:_A_Dame_to_Kill_For "Sin City: A Dame to Kill For")* in spring 2009,[[23]](#cite_note-23) and Uslan indicated that filming for *The Spirit* would begin before Miller started *Sin City 2*.[[17]](#cite_note-panel-17) Following the casting of [Gabriel Macht](/wiki/Gabriel_Macht "Gabriel Macht") as the Spirit in August 2007, filming was re-slated for the following October.[[24]](#cite_note-macht-24)

### Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Spirit_(2008_film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Casting")]

Miller held auditions for the title role, and Macht was cast in August 2007.[[24]](#cite_note-macht-24) Mendes told Miller that she wanted to work with him on *The Spirit* before she had even seen a script for the film.[[25]](#cite_note-carroll-25)

Jackson was Miller's first choice for the role of the supervillain and was cast in May 2007.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Jackson, Miller, and the costume designer developed elaborate costumes for the Octopus to wear; they are different for each of his scenes. They include a [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") robe complete with a wig, a full [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi "Nazi") *[Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel")* uniform, a [Western](/wiki/Western_(genre) "Western (genre)") duster-influenced outfit with an out-of-proportion [cowboy hat](/wiki/Cowboy_hat "Cowboy hat"), and a costume consisting of a [karakul hat](/wiki/Karakul_(hat) "Karakul (hat)") and a [fur-lined coat](/wiki/Fur_coat "Fur coat") influenced by 1970s [blaxploitation](/wiki/Blaxploitation "Blaxploitation") [pimps](/wiki/Pimp "Pimp"). When asked about the change from the Octopus being recognized in the comics by distinctive gloves, Jackson said, "It's just an opportunity to be larger than life to take the Octopus's theme of dressing the way he feels every day, or having a theme to his day to day life and making some sense with it. And hopefully, the audience will take the ride with us."[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Spirit_(2008_film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Filming")]

Filming began in October 2007, and took place at [Albuquerque Studios](/wiki/Albuquerque_Studios "Albuquerque Studios") in [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico").[[5]](#cite_note-love-5) *The Spirit* was mainly filmed with [Panavision](/wiki/Panavision "Panavision")'s [Genesis](/wiki/Genesis_(camera) "Genesis (camera)") digital camera. [High-speed photography](/wiki/High-speed_photography "High-speed photography") was filmed with [Vision Research](/wiki/Vision_Research_(company) "Vision Research (company)")'s [Phantom](/wiki/Phantom_(high-speed_camera_brand) "Phantom (high-speed camera brand)") camera.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The film's release was originally scheduled for January 16, 2009, but on May 6, 2008, it was announced that the release date would be moved up to December 25, 2008.[[17]](#cite_note-panel-17)

The film contains a number of references to Eisner collaborators and other comic book luminaries. These include "Feiffer Industrial Salt", alluding to *The Spirit* [ghost writer](/wiki/Ghost_writer "Ghost writer") [Jules Feiffer](/wiki/Jules_Feiffer "Jules Feiffer"); "Iger Avenue", named for [Eisner & Iger](/wiki/Eisner_%26_Iger "Eisner & Iger") partner [S.M. "Jerry" Iger](/wiki/Jerry_Iger "Jerry Iger"); "Ditko's Speedy Delivery", named for [Steve Ditko](/wiki/Steve_Ditko "Steve Ditko"), a comic book artist and writer; and the characters Donenfeld and Liebowitz, played by [Richard Portnow](/wiki/Richard_Portnow "Richard Portnow") and Frank Miller, respectively, who are named for two of [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics")' founders, [Harry Donenfeld](/wiki/Harry_Donenfeld "Harry Donenfeld") and [Jack Liebowitz](/wiki/Jack_Liebowitz "Jack Liebowitz").[[18]](#cite_note-filmjournal1-18)

Producer Deborah Del Prete said that Miller wanted "elements of the '40s jazz sound married with iconic heroic music and even a touch of the [spaghetti western](/wiki/Spaghetti_Western "Spaghetti Western")."[[29]](#cite_note-29) *The Spirit*'s mysterious [Henry Mancini](/wiki/Henry_Mancini "Henry Mancini")-like soundtrack was composed and conducted by [David Newman](/wiki/David_Newman_(composer) "David Newman (composer)").

> It's Sand Saref (Eva Mendes) who has the most elaborate of all the themes because it's based on her relationship with Denny Colt when they were in their teens, well before he became the Spirit. Saref's music ultimately becomes the love theme of the movie. It's very romantic, almost old fashioned, especially when they finally kiss. Frank Miller and I talked about that scene quite a bit. He really wanted me to 'go for it'—to make their music as romantic as possible. In the end, the Spirit is like a modern day [Don Juan](/wiki/Don_Juan "Don Juan"), without the psychological ambivalence towards women. He truly loves every woman he meets. It's part of his makeup. He has a certain naiveté in this respect.

— David Newman[[30]](#cite_note-30)

There is an eerie, wordless soprano for Lorelei (Jaime King) that is performed by Newman's 19-year-old daughter Diana, a vocal major at the University of Southern California.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

[Christina Aguilera](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera "Christina Aguilera") sings a cover of the classic "[Falling in Love Again](/wiki/Falling_in_Love_Again_(Can%27t_Help_It) "Falling in Love Again (Can't Help It)")" in the closing credits of *The Spirit*. The song dates to 1930, written by [Frederick Hollander](/wiki/Frederick_Hollander "Frederick Hollander"), with lyrics written by [Sammy Lerner](/wiki/Sammy_Lerner "Sammy Lerner"). The song was originally sung and popularized by [Marlene Dietrich](/wiki/Marlene_Dietrich "Marlene Dietrich") in the film *[The Blue Angel](/wiki/The_Blue_Angel "The Blue Angel")* (1930) and has been covered by [Billie Holiday](/wiki/Billie_Holiday "Billie Holiday") (1940), [Doris Day](/wiki/Doris_Day "Doris Day") (1961), and [Sammy Davis Jr.](/wiki/Sammy_Davis_Jr. "Sammy Davis Jr.") (1962).

The trailers for the film feature music from the *[I Choose Noise](/wiki/I_Choose_Noise "I Choose Noise")* album by [Hybrid](/wiki/Hybrid_(Welsh_band) "Hybrid (Welsh band)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[relevant?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Stay_on_topic "Wikipedia:Writing better articles")*]

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Spirit_(2008_film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Spirit_(2008_film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Box office")]

Released in 2,509 theaters,[[3]](#cite_note-bom-3) *The Spirit* grossed $10.3 million in its opening four days, placing 9th in the box-office ranking for the weekend.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The film grossed $19.8 million in the US and $18.6 million internationally for a worldwide total of $38.4 million.[[3]](#cite_note-bom-3) *Variety* estimated that the film's poor performance at the box office cost Odd Lot Entertainment tens of millions of dollars in losses, but Odd Lot Entertainment's CEO Gigi Pritzker denied rumors that Miller's other projects had been canceled.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Critical reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Spirit_(2008_film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Critical reception")]

On the [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), 14% of 114 critics' reviews are positive, with an average rating of 3.6/10. The website's consensus reads: "Though its visuals are unique, The Spirit's plot is almost incomprehensible, the dialogue is ludicrously mannered, and the characters are unmemorable."[[36]](#cite_note-36) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), which uses a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_arithmetic_mean "Weighted arithmetic mean"), assigned the film a score of 30 out of 100, based on 24 critics, indicating "generally unfavorable" reviews.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "C−" on an A+ to F scale.[[38]](#cite_note-cinemascore-38)

[Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") of the *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")* gave the film one out of four stars and said, "There is not a trace of human emotion in it. To call the characters cardboard is to insult a useful packing material".[[39]](#cite_note-39)

In a positive review, Ricky Bentley of the *[Miami Herald](/wiki/Miami_Herald "Miami Herald")* said, "Macht manages to meld macho with melodrama to make the Spirit come to life."[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[Frank Lovece](/wiki/Frank_Lovece "Frank Lovece") of *[Newsday](/wiki/Newsday "Newsday")*, a one-time comic-book writer, found that "gorgeous [cinematography](/wiki/Cinematography "Cinematography") and design can't mask the hollow core and bizarre ugliness of this mishandled comics adaptation", and noted that while Eisner's own Spirit was "an average-Joe [...] in a rumpled suit—a vulnerable but insouciant [everyman](/wiki/Everyman "Everyman") in humanist fables", Miller's Spirit "now has a superpower—a healing factor. Eisner's own spirit must be spinning in its grave".[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Chris Barsanti of *[Filmcritic.com](/wiki/Filmcritic.com "Filmcritic.com")* stated, "It's a frankly gorgeous effect, liberated by the fact that Miller adapted freely from Eisner's panels—the two were longtime friends—to create an organic story instead of slavishly following the master's work", and calling it "one of the year's most refreshingly fun films."[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[Owen Gleiberman](/wiki/Owen_Gleiberman "Owen Gleiberman") of *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")*, found the movie a "ludicrously knowing and mannered noir pastiche, full of burnt-end romance and 'style', but robotic at its core".[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Ken Hanke of *[Mountain Xpress](/wiki/Mountain_Xpress "Mountain Xpress")* observed, "The film may not move smoothly—Miller's too fond of 'just damn weird' digressions for that—but it does move and isn't hard to follow. Its screwiness is deliberate and it's all a matter of taste."[[44]](#cite_note-44)

[A. O. Scott](/wiki/A._O._Scott "A. O. Scott") in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* summed up, "To ask why anything happens in Frank Miller's sludgy, hyper-stylized adaptation of a fabled comic book series by Will Eisner may be an exercise in futility. The only halfway interesting question is why the thing exists at all."[[45]](#cite_note-45)

In 2010, *[Empire](/wiki/Empire_(magazine) "Empire (magazine)")* magazine listed the film at No. 32 on their "Top 50 Worst Movies of All Time" list.[[46]](#cite_note-46)
